title: Share of cancer deaths attributed to risk factors
subtitle: The estimated share of deaths from [cancers](#dod:cancer) attributed to all [risk factors](#dod:risk-factor). The remaining share represents deaths that would be expected to have occurred in the absence of these known risk factors.
caption: In 2023, the estimated share of global cancer deaths attributed to known risk factors varies widely across cancer types, ranging from nearly 100% for cervical cancer to less than 1% for prostate cancer. The horizontal bar chart displays 22 distinct cancer categories, ordered from highest to lowest attributable share. Cervical cancer sits at the top with 100% of deaths linked to risk factors, followed closely by mesothelioma at 91.7% and lung, tracheal and bronchus cancers at 76.4%. Laryngeal cancer accounts for 73.4% of deaths tied to risk factors, while esophageal and colon and rectum cancers fall in the mid-50% range — 56.4% and 56.2% respectively. Liver cancer registers at 48.2%, placing it just below the halfway mark. Mid-tier cancers include lip and oral cavity (39.4%), pancreatic (38.7%), uterine (33.3%), bladder (33%), breast (29.8%), and kidney (27.9%). Nasopharyngeal cancer stands at 23.7%, stomach at 19%, and leukemia at 18.7%. Thyroid and gallbladder and biliary tract cancers both hover near 11.5%, while multiple myeloma reaches 7.6%. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma falls to 4.8%, and prostate cancer occupies the lowest position at 0.9%. The distribution shows a steep drop after the top three categories, then a gradual decline through the middle tiers, with the bottom five cancers all falling below 12%. The visible pattern places cervical, mesothelioma, and lung-related cancers far above all others in terms of risk factor attribution, while prostate cancer remains an outlier at the extreme low end.
